metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'overlap is detected against ANY other ticket store-wide' with a recall-bounded, fixture-anchored criterion (e.g., 'recall@20 ≥ 0.9 on the S6 labeled fixture of ≥20 ticket pairs, including cross-epic and non-epic pairs') so the criterion is evaluable in-session.
findings.finding: Success Criterion 1 sets 'overlap is detected against ANY other ticket store-wide' as the measurability bar, but 'ANY other ticket' is an absolute/exhaustive standard that is not evaluable in-session. The derisking section validates recall@20=1.0 on N=8 paraphrases as the concrete metric, but this bound is never imported into the Success Criteria or AC, leaving the criterion without a testable threshold.
findings.location: Success Criteria, bullet 1: 'For the ticket under review, overlap is detected against ANY other ticket store-wide (cross-epic AND non-epic)'
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the validated recall@20 metric from the derisking section: 'On the S6 labeled fixture (≥20 pairs, cross-epic and non-epic), recall@20 ≥ 0.9 and the related-but-distinct pair is not surfaced.' This mirrors the derisking evidence and is deterministic to run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the behavioral distinction between `overlap_drain = 'async'` and `overlap_drain = 'always'` (e.g., `async` = spawn drain only when soaked entries are found; `always` = always spawn a drain child unconditionally) and add at least one AC covering a non-default non-off value.
findings.finding: The `overlap_drain` STRING enum (`off|async|always`) is introduced as an LLMConfig tunable, but the semantic distinction between `async` and `always` is never defined in the plan — an executor cannot implement or test the two non-off values.
findings.location: Config placement section — 'drain mode/batch' listed as LLM-feature tunables on LLMConfig
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence definition per value in the Design or Config placement section: `off` = never drain; `async` = detach only when the soaked-entry check finds pending digests; `always` = always spawn a drain subprocess unconditionally (useful for CI/manual flush).

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a latency and cost estimate (or experiment result) for the LLM pairwise judge phase (up to K=20 candidates) to the 'Derisking experiments' section, define what 'bounded' means for the judge (e.g., max-K calls, per-invocation token cap, per-pair token limit), and confirm the overall store-wide step latency/cost envelope fits the plan-review invocation window.
findings.finding: The plan provides experiment-validated latency and cost data for the BM25F retrieval stage (0.69ms at 800 digests) and the enrichment stage ($0.0024/ticket), but gives no analogous budget for the LLM pairwise judge phase — up to 20 candidate pairs judged per review invocation. The claim that this runs 'during the plan-review invocation window' is therefore partially ungrounded: the BM25F leg is 14× under the 10ms target, but the judge leg (up to 20 bounded LLM calls) could dominate latency and cost by several orders of magnitude. Without a stated budget for the judge phase, the overall latency and cost envelope of the store-wide step is unknown.
findings.location: Design section: 'Derisking experiments … Latency: VALIDATED. brute-force BM25F single-query top-20 = 0.69 ms at 800 digests (~14× under the 10 ms budget)'; 'Enrichment economics: VALIDATED. ~$0.0024/ticket'; 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate and surfaces ≤3 advisory link suggestions'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step (or assign to an existing child) to update the operator-facing config reference (README, docs/ config guide, or `_config_schema.py` docstrings) with `overlap_enabled` and all `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` tunables, their types, defaults, and effects — distinct from the architectural ADR delivered in S7.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new operator-facing config surface — `overlap_enabled` on `VerifyConfig` and a family of `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` tunables on `LLMConfig` — but does not mention updating any user/operator configuration reference documentation (e.g., README, docs/ config guide, or inline schema docstrings) beyond the architectural ADR. The ADR captures architectural rationale, not the operator knob reference; if a config reference doc exists, it will be stale after S1–S6 land.
findings.location: Config placement section; AC line 'Runs as a store-wide step during the plan-review window'
findings.suggested_fix: Assign to S7 (alongside the ADR) or to the earliest child that introduces a tunable: add a section to docs/ (or expand the ADR) that lists every new config key, its type, default, allowed values, and effect. At minimum, add docstrings to the new `VerifyConfig` and `LLMConfig` fields so `rebar config --help` or source inspection is self-documenting.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the data-handling posture for the bulk async enrichment path in the ADR (S7): which LLM provider endpoint receives ticket content, whether provider data-retention/training opt-outs apply, and whether an exclusion/opt-out label exists for tickets that must not leave the local trust boundary.
findings.finding: The plan introduces bulk, store-wide async LLM enrichment of ALL tickets (not just the one under review) but does not declare the data-handling posture for this new, broader data-egress pattern — what ticket content is transmitted, to which LLM provider endpoint, and whether any ticket categories (e.g., containing credentials, PII, or embargoed roadmap items) should be excluded from enrichment.
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)' + 'Enrichment economics: VALIDATED. ~$0.0024/ticket, ≈ $1.90 one-time for all 793 tickets'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the S7 ADR a 'Data egress contract' section stating: (1) the LLM provider endpoint used, (2) that the provider's zero-retention / no-training API tier (or equivalent opt-out) is configured, (3) a `rebar.toml` opt-out label (e.g., `overlap_exclude = true`) that suppresses enrichment for sensitive tickets, and (4) that the enrichment queue drains only ticket fields already visible in the local store (no credential expansion).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] 0f70 (S7) must declare an explicit ordering dependency on c1de (S4) because both edit `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py`.
findings.finding: 0f70 (S7) edits `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` for `_render_plan_review_text`, and c1de (S4) also edits `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` for the `rebar enrich` CLI arm — but 0f70 documents only a depends_on S3 ordering constraint (for `plan_review/__init__.py`) and does NOT declare an ordering dependency on c1de (S4). These two children edit the same file without a sequencing guard, creating a merge-conflict risk that is undocumented.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `depends_on c1de` to 0f70's Dependencies section alongside the existing `depends_on S3`, mirroring how the S3 ordering constraint is already documented, so the landing sequence is S4 → S7 for that file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] e1f4 (S3) must add a `test_older_binary_ignores` test (analogous to 2d0f's) verifying that ENQUEUE_ENRICH/CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH are preserved-and-ignored with no fsck WARN by an older-version reader.
findings.finding: e1f4 (S3) adds three new event types (`ENQUEUE_ENRICH`, `CLAIM_ENRICH`, `DONE_ENRICH`) to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (sidecar/preserved-and-ignored semantics) but its test list and ACs include no `test_older_binary_ignores` equivalent, unlike sibling 2d0f (S2) which explicitly tests that an older binary preserves-and-ignores `TICKET_DIGEST` with no fsck WARN. The backward-compatibility guarantee for the queue event types is therefore untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `test_older_binary_ignores` to `tests/unit/test_enrich_queue.py` that simulates a store containing queue events and verifies (a) `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` membership prevents SNAPSHOT compaction and (b) fsck emits no WARN for unknown-to-old-binary events — mirroring `tests/unit/test_ticket_digest_store.py::test_older_binary_ignores`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the initial default value for each LLM-layer tunable in the Config placement section so child-story executors have a concrete starting point (e.g., K=20, conf_threshold=0.7, surface_cap=3, max_doc_freq=0.9, drain mode=opportunistic).
findings.finding: The LLM-layer tunables (proposition bounds, BM25F field weights, K, max_doc_freq, conf_threshold, surface_cap, soak, lease, drain mode/batch) are named and config-located but carry no stated default values. An executor building any of S1–S7 must choose these defaults without guidance from the plan.
findings.location: Config placement section: 'ALL other tunables (proposition bounds, BM25F weights, K, max_doc_freq, conf_threshold, surface_cap, soak, lease, drain mode/batch) are LLM-feature tunables and live on LLMConfig'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Tunable defaults' sub-table to the Config placement section listing each param, its default, and a one-line rationale (e.g., K=20 matches the recall-validated BM25F top-20 window). Executors may override via REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_* env vars but have a defined starting point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion covering S6's precision-measurement deliverable: e.g. 'A labeled fixture of ticket pairs (including at least one related-but-distinct pair) is delivered, and precision on that fixture meets the defined threshold; a related-but-distinct pair is not surfaced as an overlap candidate.' (mirrors SC3 so every Success Criterion has a covering AC).
findings.finding: Success Criterion SC3 — 'precision is measured on a labeled fixture of ticket pairs (S6), and a related-but-distinct pair must not be surfaced' — is an explicit deliverable (labeled fixture + precision measurement, attributed to S6) that appears in Success Criteria but has no covering Acceptance Criterion. None of AC1–AC4 verifies or mentions a precision fixture, a labeled pair dataset, or an S6-owned measurement step.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] A labeled fixture of related-but-distinct ticket pairs is delivered (S6); the pairwise judge does not surface a related-but-distinct pair as a duplicate/supersedes/depends_on candidate on that fixture (precision gate).'
This directly mirrors SC3 and closes the orphan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavior when the query ticket's Cupid digest is absent at invocation time (e.g., synthesize synchronously as a one-off with a latency/cost note in the advisory output; emit an explicit 'overlap detection skipped — digest not yet available' advisory; or block enrichment synchronously for the query ticket only), and assign ownership to a named child story (S1–S7).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the behavior when the QUERY ticket itself has no cached Cupid digest at invocation time — the most likely failure mode for a brand-new ticket on its first plan review. The enrichment queue is described as async/opportunistic ('Tier 1'), but the overlap check runs synchronously 'during the plan-review invocation window (before claim).' If the digest for the ticket under review has not yet been drained from the queue, the BM25F stage has no query vector to issue, and the system's behavior (skip silently, synthesize synchronously, emit a caveat) is unspecified. This is a hidden ordering assumption that breaks for precisely the case where overlap detection is most valuable: a new ticket being reviewed for the first time.
findings.location: Design section: 'Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank: (0) each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest … (1) brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates … (2) a bounded LLM pairwise judge'; also 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, add a 'query-ticket bootstrap' rule: when the query ticket has no cached digest at invocation time, synthesize it synchronously (a single LLM extract call, same as the async path) before issuing the BM25F query, and note the on-demand cost in the advisory output. This keeps the 'async for corpus enrichment, sync for the query ticket' invariant explicit and assigns a predictable cost ceiling to the single synchronous call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the specific Python library (or commit to an inline stdlib implementation) that provides BM25F field weighting, and reconcile it with the 'no new dependency' constraint — the experiment that validated recall@20=1.0 must have used the same mechanism the child stories will implement.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'BM25F' with field weighting (title/problem-statement weighted high, max_doc_freq, stopwords) but never identifies the Python library that implements BM25F, while simultaneously asserting 'NO embeddings, NO vector DB, NO new dependency.' The standard `rank_bm25` pip package (the most common BM25 library for Python) implements BM25Okapi, BM25L, and BM25Plus — not BM25F (which requires per-field separate index and combination formula). BM25F is not present in pyproject.toml under any extra. No inline implementation is committed to. This leaves the field-weighting mechanism — load-bearing for the recall result ('title/problem-statement weighted high') — as an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: Design section: 'Stage 1 — candidate generation … BM25F "More Like This" of the query ticket's digest against the corpus digests (title/problem-statement weighted high; max_doc_freq + stopwords prune boilerplate)'; also Derisking experiments: 'Store primitives: CONFIRMED … no new dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, either (a) name the library (e.g. `bm25s`, `rank_bm25` BM25Okapi with manual per-field scoring, or a custom inline implementation) and add it to `pyproject.toml` under the appropriate extra, or (b) explicitly confirm the experiment used plain BM25Okapi with a field-weight wrapper and document that the inline implementation reproduces it — so child story S5 has a concrete, testable target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (a) an idempotency guard for the queue-drain initial fill (skip-if-already-digested and non-stale), so re-running after interruption is safe; and (b) a batch size / rate-limit / concurrency cap for the initial corpus population pass, so operators can control API spend and avoid rate-limit failures at scale.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges a one-time corpus-wide backfill of ~793 existing tickets with LLM-extracted digests but specifies no batch size, rate limit, or throttling contract for that initial population pass, and never asserts the queue-drain operation is idempotent/resumable if interrupted mid-corpus.
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment economics: VALIDATED. ~$0.0024/ticket, ≈ $1.90 one-time for all 793 tickets, cached and re-run only on edit.' and 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1).'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section under 'Enrichment economics', add: (1) 'The enqueue step skips tickets that already have a fresh, non-stale digest (idempotent: re-running the initial fill is safe).' (2) 'The initial backfill drain is throttled to at most N concurrent LLM calls (configurable via REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN_BATCH, default N=10) to bound API rate-limit exposure and allow cost-controlled catch-up.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the post-rollback schema state explicitly: confirm digest sidecars are (a) a recognized-but-ignored event type in the reducer's _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (or equivalent) so older clones preserve-and-ignore them, and (b) safe to leave in place after turning off `overlap_enabled`, or specify a cleanup/prune path if they are not.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema STATE after rollback — turning off `overlap_enabled` presumably stops new digests from being generated, but the plan is silent on whether the already-persisted digest sidecars are left in place, cleaned up, or ignored by reducers/fsck, and whether leaving them is safe for older clones.
findings.location: Config placement section — '`overlap_enabled` (bool) lives on `VerifyConfig`'; Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria — no rollback/back-out path stated for the digest corpus.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Design or Acceptance Criteria: 'Digest sidecars are registered in `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (or the equivalent forward-compat list) so older clones preserve-and-ignore them; turning off `overlap_enabled` is the complete behavioral rollback — existing digest sidecars are left in place and harmlessly ignored.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete BM25F library (e.g., `rank_bm25`, `bm25s`) or state that BM25F will be implemented from scratch, and add the library to `pyproject.toml` (under the appropriate extra, likely `[agents]`) — so the child stories implementing Stage 1 have an unambiguous integration target whose license, API surface, and tokenization behavior can be verified against the experiment setup.
findings.finding: BM25F is the load-bearing retrieval mechanism (Stage 1 of the two-stage pipeline) and the plan reports validated experiment results (recall@20=1.0, 0.69ms at 800 digests), but neither the plan nor the codebase names or ships a BM25 library. No BM25 dependency appears in pyproject.toml (neither in core, [agents], [grounding], nor any other extra), and no BM25/rank_bm25/bm25s/bm25f source is present anywhere under src/. The plan's claim that 'No embedding stage is needed' and 'no ANN / vector DB' are validated — but those validations are premised on BM25F working correctly, which requires a concrete implementation that has not been identified.
findings.location: Design section — 'Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank: … (1) brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'; Derisking experiments — 'Recall: VALIDATED. digest-BM25F retrieved 8/8 heavy paraphrases at rank 1, recall@20 = 1.0 … Latency: VALIDATED. brute-force BM25F single-query top-20 = 0.69 ms at 800 digests'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, replace 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests' with the specific library or a note like 'implemented in-repo (no external dep)'. If using `rank_bm25` (the most common Python BM25 library), add it to pyproject.toml and note that field weighting (BM25F vs. plain BM25) must be verified against the experiment's recall numbers since `rank_bm25` provides BM25Okapi/BM25Plus/BM25L variants, not BM25F natively.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the pairwise judge output schema (field names, classification label enum, confidence/score type, abstain semantics) and co-locate the `conf_threshold` decision rule with it so S6 and S7 implementors share a single contract.
findings.finding: The LLM pairwise judge (Stage 2) 'classifies each candidate' into a relation type, but the output schema of that judge call is never defined: no JSON structure, no field names, no classification label enum (what are the legal values — 'duplicate', 'supersedes', 'depends_on', 'unrelated', 'abstain'?), no confidence/score field definition, and no schema co-location with the `conf_threshold` decision rule that consumes it.
findings.location: Design section — 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate and surfaces ≤3 advisory link suggestions'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Judge output schema' subsection: e.g. `{relation: 'duplicate'|'supersedes'|'depends_on'|'unrelated', confidence: float [0,1], rationale: str}` with a decision rule: surface iff `relation != 'unrelated' AND confidence >= conf_threshold`; abstain (confidence undefined) drops to 'unrelated'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the Cupid digest schema (field names, types, and the `propositions` structure the bounds govern) and name the BM25F-weighted fields so the retriever and enrichment op share a common contract.
findings.finding: The 'Cupid digest' is the load-bearing cached artifact for BM25F retrieval, but its schema is never defined: no field names, no types, no JSON shape, and no specification of what the `overlap_propositions_min`/`max` bounds actually constrain (a `propositions` array of what? strings? objects?).
findings.location: Design section — '(0) each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest (no embeddings, no vector DB)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Digest schema' subsection, e.g.: `{ticket_id: str, title: str, summary: str, propositions: list[str], tags: list[str]}` with `overlap_propositions_min`/`max` bounding `len(propositions)` and BM25F weights applied to `title`, `summary`, `propositions`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure fallback for a failed enrichment LLM call: define the retry policy (or explicit no-retry), what the retriever does when a digest is absent (skip ticket / use raw text / log and continue), and the maximum staleness or retry horizon for DONE_ENRICH.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for LLM enrichment sub-step failure: the plan says enrichment is async and cached, but does not define what happens when the LLM call for a digest fails (network error, rate limit, malformed output, or budget exhaustion) — does the entry stay unenriched in the queue indefinitely, retry with backoff, or cause the overlap check to silently skip that ticket?
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `overlap[]` item schema (required fields: at minimum `candidate_id`, `relation`, `confidence`, `link_command`) in the plan and commit to adding it to `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` before S7 ships.
findings.finding: The `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` is confirmed in the codebase with no `overlap[]` property; the plan says this field will be added but never defines the overlap item schema (field names, required fields, what the `rebar link` command string field is called), leaving the S7 rendering step without a contract.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Surfaces ≤3 candidate duplicate/supersede/dependency relations for human confirmation with copy-paste `rebar link` commands'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Overlap item schema' subsection: `{candidate_id: str, relation: 'duplicates'|'supersedes'|'depends_on', confidence: float, rationale: str, link_command: str}` where `link_command` is a fully-formed `rebar link <query_id> <candidate_id> <relation>` string.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
